name: Syria
avoid: Strong safety advisory: Syria remains a post-conflict destination with checkpoints, rebuilding damage, sudden district-level restrictions, and uneven services; only travel after checking current government advisories, using a vetted local operator or fixer, and keeping every leg flexible.

Do not improvise border crossings, independent night transfers, or remote detours around Aleppo, Palmyra, Homs, or the western valleys because road conditions and access rules can change with little notice.

Card acceptance, ATMs, medical support, and hotel reopening status can all be inconsistent, so carry enough cash, keep offline maps and document copies, and reconfirm every hotel and transport booking directly.
stopoverNote: A 5- to 6-day Syria loop works only if you keep it tight around Damascus, Aleppo, Palmyra, and Krak des Chevaliers with pre-arranged daylight transport and enough slack for checkpoint or closure delays.

cities.name: Damascus
cities.notes: Old Damascus, the Umayyad Mosque, Al-Hamidiyah Souq, Azm Palace, and Christian Quarter lanes make this the essential arrival base.

cities.name: Aleppo
cities.notes: Aleppo Citadel, Al-Madina Souq, Jdeideh heritage lanes, and some of Syria's strongest food traditions anchor the north.

cities.name: Palmyra
cities.notes: Tadmur is the base for Palmyra's Roman-era ruins, desert viewpoints, and guide-led archaeological visits.

cities.name: Krak des Chevaliers
cities.notes: The crusader castle above Al-Husn is Syria's most dramatic fortress stop and works best as a western-valley day trip or overnight.

itinerary.sim: Syriatel or MTN Syria prepaid SIMs usually land around ₹300–₹900 for enough data for WhatsApp, offline maps, and hotel coordination, but coverage and eSIM support are inconsistent outside the main city cores.

Damascus.name: Damascus
Damascus.note: Use Damascus to settle SIM, cash, and local guidance first; the Old City is the easiest place to sightsee on foot, while longer transfers should stay daylight-only.

days.theme: Walk the UNESCO core through Syria's best-known mosque, market lanes, and classic Old City tables

activities.name: Umayyad Mosque Damascus
activities.tip: Dress modestly and avoid main prayer windows; tourist entry is usually priced separately from local worship access.

activities.name: Old City of Damascus and Al-Hamidiyah Souq walk
Mausoleum of Saladin
Bakdash ice cream or early mezze stop

days.theme: Add Ottoman-house museums, Christian Quarter streets, and a second slow-food Damascus day

activities.name: Azm Palace
Straight Street and Bab Sharqi walk
Khan As'ad Pasha exterior and nearby souq lanes
Courtyard meal at Beit Jabri

days.theme: Keep a third Damascus day only for lighter heritage stops and buffer time before the northbound transfer

activities.name: National Museum of Damascus
Tekkiyeh Suleimaniyah handicrafts market
Short taxi to Mount Qasioun overlook

Aleppo.name: Aleppo
Aleppo.note: Only head north with current local advice; the historic core is the goal, and you should avoid improvising late-evening arrivals or extra district hopping.

days.theme: Focus on the citadel, souk spine, and Aleppo's classic mansion restaurants in one concentrated heritage day

activities.name: Aleppo Citadel
activities.tip: Some sections can be closed for restoration, so buy the ticket only after confirming same-day access.

activities.name: Al-Madina Souq walk
Great Mosque of Aleppo courtyard and old market lanes
Dinner at Sissi House Restaurant

days.theme: Add a second Aleppo day for museum time, Jdeideh, and slower café pacing

activities.name: Aleppo National Museum
Jdeideh quarter walk
Tea or coffee at Baron Hotel

Palmyra.name: Palmyra
Palmyra.note: Treat Palmyra as a guide-led daylight archaeology leg from Tadmur; access, restoration zones, and where you can actually stay can all change fast.

days.theme: See the famous desert ruins with time for the colonnade, monumental remains, and a cautious sunset return

activities.name: Palmyra ruins main archaeological site
activities.tip: Sections can be partially closed for restoration or security, so expect the exact walking loop to vary.

activities.name: Great Colonnade and Monumental Arch area
Temple of Bel precinct exterior viewpoints
activities.cost: Included with Palmyra site entry if accessible

activities.name: Hotel dinner in Tadmur

days.theme: Keep a second Palmyra day only for extra viewpoints, a local guide buffer, and museum-status-dependent stops

activities.name: Qalaat Ibn Maan viewpoint by shared taxi or driver
Valley of the Tombs area viewpoints
Local guide contribution for archaeology context

Krak des Chevaliers.name: Krak des Chevaliers
Krak des Chevaliers.note: This western-valley stop works best with a driver or carefully timed shared taxi because onward public transport thins out fast after lunch.

days.theme: Give Syria's headline crusader fortress a full daylight block with nearby village views and a proper lunch stop

activities.name: Krak des Chevaliers
activities.tip: Carry small cash because ticketing is usually simple and card payment should not be expected.

activities.name: Ramparts, chapel, and inner halls circuit
activities.cost: Included with Krak des Chevaliers entry

activities.name: Al-Husn village and valley viewpoint walk
Lunch or coffee at Al Wadi Hotel

connections.method: Domestic bus or shared taxi by day; private driver is the easiest fallback if schedules shift
Domestic bus or shared taxi, usually split via Homs if no direct seat is running
Domestic bus or shared taxi via Homs, with a private car as the more reliable same-day option

extras: Travel advisory: Syria is still a post-conflict destination. Recheck official advisories before departure and again before every overland leg because checkpoints, local permissions, and site access can change faster than hotel listings update.

Umayyad Mosque is the main foreign-visitor-priced heritage ticket in this route, while Aleppo Citadel, Palmyra, and Krak des Chevaliers are usually inexpensive SYP-denominated entries, which is why their INR equivalents look unusually low.
Domestic buses and shared taxis are the default public-transport backbone, but they are cash-first, daylight-oriented, and sometimes leave only when full; never plan a tight evening connection.

Aleppo, Palmyra, and western-valley hotel inventory can be fluid after years of disruption, so treat every named stay as a real reference point but reconfirm current operating status directly before paying.
